lessonTitle: Beware Lest Thou Forget the Lord
quickInsight: When life is comfortable, that's when you're most likely to drift from God.

quotableVerses.text: And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might.
I call heaven and earth to record this day against you, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life, that both thou and thy seed may live.

And thou shalt remember all the way which the LORD thy God led thee these forty years in the wilderness, to humble thee, and to prove thee, to know what was in thine heart, whether thou wouldest keep his commandments, or no.
Beware that thou forget not the LORD thy God, in not keeping his commandments, and his judgments, and his statutes, which I command thee this day.

sections.title: The Story
items: Moses is old. He's spent 40 years leading millions of people through a desert, and now he's standing at the edge of the promised land — a place he'll never set foot in.
This is his final speech. Everything he's ever wanted to say to Israel, he says now. It's not battle tactics or survival tips — it's 'Don't forget God when things get easy.'
He reminds them of everything God did: plagues in Egypt, the Red Sea, manna every morning. Then he warns: when you're comfortable, fed, and settled — that's when you'll forget.
Moses dies on Mount Nebo, looking out over a land he gave his life to reach. His faithfulness still got him to God's real promised land — eternal life.

sections.title: Why This Hits Different at 14
items: Moses isn't warning about hard times — he's warning about easy ones. When life is good, when you're killing it in sports or your friend group is solid, that's when God quietly slides to the back of your mind.

Think about your phone. You charge it every night without thinking. But if you stopped charging it, you wouldn't notice for a while — until it dies at the worst moment. Your connection to God works the same way.
Deuteronomy 8:17 warns about saying 'My power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth.' It's easy to take credit for your wins and forget who actually blessed you with those talents.
This chapter is basically God saying: 'I'm not worried about you when life is hard — that's when you'll pray. I'm worried about you when everything's fine.'

sections.title: Heart Check
items: The manual frames this week as a spiritual checkup on your heart. Ask the boys: if God could see a dashboard of what's in your heart right now — what you think about, what you want, what you're chasing — what would it show?

Draw a heart on the board. Inside write what Deuteronomy says should be there (love for God, His commandments, gratitude). Outside write what creeps in (pride, distraction, the 'I got this on my own' attitude).

Object lesson: hand a boy your phone (locked). Ask him to unlock it. He can't. Give him the passcode and he's in. Point: God's commandments aren't locks keeping you out — they're the passcode to everything He wants to give you.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: When's a time things were going well and you realized you hadn't prayed or read scriptures in a while? What pulled you back?
Moses says love God with all your heart, soul, and might. What does 'all your might' look like for a 14-year-old? What would actually change this week?
If you had to explain to a friend who's not a member why God matters to you — no church voice, just real — what would you say?
Deuteronomy 30:19 says 'choose life.' What does choosing spiritual life over spiritual death look like on a random Tuesday after school?

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Every night this week, before you charge your phone, name one specific thing God did for you that day. Out loud. Not in your head — say it.

Pick one verse from Deuteronomy 6:5–9 and put it somewhere you'll see daily — lock screen, bathroom mirror, locker. The Israelites literally tied scriptures to their foreheads. You can handle a sticky note.

sections.title: Restoration Connection
items: Deuteronomy 18:15–19 prophesies a 'Prophet like unto Moses.' The Savior told the Nephites directly: 'I am he of whom Moses spake' (3 Nephi 20:23). Peter, Nephi, and Moroni all referenced this same prophecy.

Lehi's final words in 2 Nephi 2:26–29 echo Moses's 'choose life' speech — choose liberty and eternal life through Christ, or captivity and death. Same doctrine, 600 years later, from a father who also wouldn't see the promised land.
The sacrament prayers (D&C 20:77) are your weekly Deuteronomy 6:12 moment — 'lest thou forget.' Every Sunday you renew the exact kind of covenant Moses begged Israel to keep.
